questions.question: Frontend load balancing at global scale typically combines DNS-based distribution with:
options: Anycast or geo-proximity routing so clients reach nearby healthy points of presence
A single monolithic server in one datacenter to simplify routing
Client-side random selection with no health awareness
Eliminating health checks to reduce DNS query volume
questions.explanation: Chapter 19 describes layered frontend LB: DNS directs clients toward regional entry points, often via anycast or geo-DNS, with health-aware routing to avoid sending users to failed locations.

questions.question: During a regional outage, DNS TTL of 300 seconds delays traffic shift. Which mitigation does chapter 19 discuss?
options: Combine low TTLs where feasible with anycast or proxy layers that can redirect traffic faster than DNS propagation alone
Increase TTL to 86400 seconds to reduce resolver load during outages
Rely solely on clients to cache stale DNS indefinitely
Disable DNS entirely and require users to bookmark IP addresses
questions.explanation: DNS caching limits failover speed. Chapter 19 advocates layered approaches — anycast, GFE/proxy tiers, controlled TTLs — because DNS alone cannot instantly drain a failed region.

questions.question: A frontend load balancer receives health signals indicating one cluster is degraded but not fully down. Capacity-aware routing should:
options: Reduce traffic sent to the degraded cluster proportionally while maintaining service via healthier clusters
Send all traffic to the degraded cluster to 'help it recover'
Mark the cluster healthy because partial degradation is normal
Drain all clusters equally regardless of health state
questions.explanation: Chapter 19 emphasizes health- and capacity-aware frontend routing: steer away from struggling clusters before they fail completely, preserving user experience across the global footprint.

questions.question: Datacenter load balancers often use consistent hashing for stateful connection affinity because:
options: It minimizes remapping when backend servers are added or removed compared to naive modulo hashing
It guarantees zero connection loss during any server failure
It eliminates the need for health checks on backend pools
It requires clients to reconnect on every request for fairness
questions.explanation: Chapter 20 covers datacenter LB algorithms. Consistent hashing limits disruption when the backend set changes — only keys near the changed node remap — important for caches and connection state.

questions.question: A proxy load balancer maintains persistent connections to backends while clients use short-lived HTTP requests. The primary reliability benefit is:
options: Amortizing connection setup cost and enabling better backend load visibility without per-request TCP handshakes
Eliminating all backend failures because connections never close
Preventing clients from retrying failed requests
Removing the need for TLS on client-facing connections
questions.explanation: Chapter 20 describes connection management at the datacenter LB layer: pooling reduces latency and CPU overhead, while the proxy observes backend health and distributes load with full connection context.

questions.question: When a frontend must fan out to thousands of backend tasks, subset selection in the load balancer addresses:
options: Limiting each client proxy to a manageable backend subset, reducing connection state and health-check overhead while preserving load spread
Sending every request to every backend simultaneously for redundancy
Eliminating load balancing by using broadcast
Increasing fan-out so each proxy tracks all backends globally
questions.explanation: Chapter 20's subsetting pattern trades perfect global optimality for scalability: each LB instance tracks a subset of backends, avoiding O(n) connection and health-check costs at massive scale.

questions.question: Under overload, chapter 21 recommends load shedding based on:
options: Request priority and criticality, dropping or degrading low-priority work before core user paths fail entirely
Random rejection of exactly 50% of all requests regardless of type
First-in-first-out acceptance until hardware physically fails
Shedding only internal monitoring traffic while accepting unbounded user load
questions.explanation: Chapter 21 on handling overload advocates deliberate shedding with priority classes — protect revenue-critical and user-visible paths, drop deferrable work — rather than uncontrolled collapse.

questions.question: A service detects queue wait time exceeding its SLO-derived threshold. The overload response aligned with chapter 21 is:
options: Reject new requests early with explicit error codes rather than accepting work that will miss latency targets
Accept all requests and rely on client timeouts to shed load
Disable autoscaling to prevent additional instances from joining
Increase retry rates from clients to improve success probability
questions.explanation: Graceful degradation under overload means failing fast with clear signals (429/503). Chapter 21 contrasts this with slow failure where queues grow, memory exhausts, and cascading outages follow.

questions.question: Why does chapter 21 argue against running services at 100% utilization under steady state?
options: Zero headroom eliminates capacity to absorb latency variance and traffic spikes, turning minor perturbations into SLO breaches
100% utilization improves tail latency by maximizing throughput
Autoscaling makes headroom unnecessary at all timescales
High utilization reduces the need for load shedding logic
questions.explanation: Operating at maximum utilization removes buffer for bursts and dependency slowdowns. Chapter 21 ties overload design to deliberate headroom and shedding policies rather than running hot continuously.

questions.question: When shedding load, returning Retry-After headers alongside 503 responses primarily helps:
options: Signal clients to back off cooperatively, reducing retry amplification during partial overload recovery
Guarantee the same request succeeds on immediate retry
Hide overload conditions from monitoring systems
Force clients to switch to a different protocol permanently
questions.explanation: Chapter 21 emphasizes cooperative degradation: explicit backoff signals help well-behaved clients avoid retry storms that would prevent recovery — linking overload handling to cascading-failure prevention.

questions.question: Chapter 22 defines a cascading failure as:
options: A failure in one component triggering overload or errors in dependents, propagating through positive feedback until the system reaches a new stable failure state
Any single-server crash that is replaced automatically by the load balancer
A planned maintenance window affecting one availability zone
A gradual performance improvement as load increases
questions.explanation: Chapter 22 distinguishes ordinary single-component failure from cascades: positive feedback loops (retries, queue growth, resource exhaustion) spread failure across otherwise healthy components.

questions.question: A retry budget limits the fraction of backend capacity that may be consumed by retries. Its purpose in cascade prevention is:
options: Capping retry amplification so recovery attempts cannot multiply load beyond what the degraded system can sustain
Eliminating all retries to maximize success rate on first attempt
Allowing unlimited retries for premium users only
Replacing health checks with retry counters
questions.explanation: Chapter 22's retry budgets address a distinct angle from generic backoff: even well-jittered retries can overwhelm a recovering backend. Budgets enforce a hard ceiling on retry traffic as a fraction of total capacity.

questions.question: Bulkhead partitioning in a multi-tenant service primarily prevents:
options: One tenant's traffic spike or failure mode from exhausting shared thread pools, connections, or memory used by other tenants
All cross-tenant data access for compliance reasons only
Autoscaling from adding capacity to individual tenants
Monitoring from attributing errors to specific tenants
questions.explanation: Chapter 22 bulkheads isolate resource pools per tenant or subsystem — a cascade-containment pattern distinct from circuit breakers. One tenant's retry storm cannot drain the shared pool that serves everyone.

questions.question: A system recovers from an initial trigger but remains in a degraded metastable state because load now exceeds reduced capacity. Chapter 22 recommends:
options: Temporarily reduce offered load (shedding, throttling clients) until the system returns to a stable operating point below saturation
Immediately restore full traffic because the initial trigger has cleared
Add retries globally to push through the degraded state faster
Disable bulkheads to maximize resource sharing during recovery
questions.explanation: Metastable failures persist after the trigger is gone because the system cannot serve full load at its degraded capacity. Chapter 22 stresses deliberate load reduction — not just fixing the root trigger — to exit the bad equilibrium.

questions.question: Chapter 22 notes that server overload protection should trigger before CPU reaches 100% because:
options: By the time CPU saturates, queues and latency have already degraded, and recovery requires shedding load not just adding CPU
CPU at 100% always causes immediate kernel panic
Load balancers cannot route to servers above 50% CPU
100% CPU improves request prioritization automatically
questions.explanation: Cascade prevention is proactive: overload signals (latency, queue depth, error rate) should trigger shedding before hard resource exhaustion. At 100% CPU the system is often already in a metastable failure mode.

questions.question: Paxos guarantees safety (consistency) with how many replicas tolerating f simultaneous failures in the standard 2f+1 configuration?
options: f failures with 2f+1 total replicas
f failures with f+1 total replicas
2f failures with f total replicas
Any number of failures with three replicas regardless of f
questions.explanation: Chapter 23 explains Paxos requires a quorum majority: 2f+1 replicas tolerate f failures while preserving agreement. This generic consensus foundation underpins many distributed systems without being Spanner-specific.

questions.question: In Multi-Paxos, electing a stable leader primarily reduces:
options: The number of round-trip phases required per operation by avoiding full Paxos prepare rounds on every write
The total number of replicas required for quorum
The need for any persistent storage on replicas
Network partitions by eliminating minority partitions
questions.explanation: Chapter 23 describes Multi-Paxos optimization: a stable leader skips repeated leader election and prepare phases for steady-state writes, improving throughput while preserving Paxos safety guarantees.

questions.question: Paxos can guarantee safety during a network partition but may sacrifice liveness when:
options: A quorum cannot be assembled, preventing progress until enough replicas communicate
Any single replica reboots regardless of quorum status
Clients use idempotent request IDs
Leaders rotate on a fixed schedule every second
questions.explanation: Chapter 23 distinguishes safety (never returning inconsistent results) from liveness (eventually making progress). Without quorum, Paxos correctly halts rather than violating consistency — a key interview distinction.

questions.question: Replicated state machines built on consensus logs ensure:
options: All non-faulty replicas apply the same ordered operations, producing identical state given deterministic execution
Replicas may diverge arbitrarily as long as one is designated primary
Order of operations is irrelevant if all replicas eventually receive all messages
Only the leader's state matters; followers are never read from
questions.explanation: Chapter 23's replicated state machine pattern: consensus orders a log; each replica applies entries deterministically. Identical inputs and order yield identical state — the generic model behind many distributed databases.

questions.question: Chapter 24's cron at scale problem arises when:
options: Many jobs scheduled at the same wall-clock time simultaneously hammer shared dependencies such as databases or lock services
Cron jobs run too infrequently to be useful
Jobs execute on only one machine globally by design
Cron daemons cannot log their execution history
questions.explanation: Chapter 24 describes thundering herd from synchronized cron schedules — distinct from cache stampede (B7) because the trigger is temporal coordination of batch jobs, not cache expiry.

questions.question: To mitigate cron-induced thundering herds, chapter 24 recommends:
options: Adding randomized jitter to scheduled start times and spreading work across a time window
Running all jobs at exactly midnight UTC for operational simplicity
Disabling monitoring during cron windows
Consolidating all cron jobs into a single monolithic script
questions.explanation: Jitter and windowed scheduling spread load over time, preventing synchronized spikes. Chapter 24 also discusses distributed cron systems with leader election and work partitioning.

questions.question: In a distributed cron system with at-least-once execution semantics, jobs must be:
options: Idempotent or guarded with external locking so duplicate runs do not corrupt state
Non-idempotent by design to detect duplicate scheduling
Restricted to run longer than the scheduling interval
Executed only on the datacenter master clock node
questions.explanation: Chapter 24's distributed cron designs assume failures and duplicates. Idempotent job design or distributed locks prevent double billing, double emails, or duplicate data mutations.

questions.question: Most large-scale data pipelines prefer at-least-once delivery with idempotent sinks because:
options: Exactly-once end-to-end is often impractical at scale; idempotent processing makes duplicates safe
At-most-once delivery guarantees no data loss
Exactly-once requires no coordination overhead in distributed systems
Idempotent sinks eliminate the need for monitoring pipeline lag
questions.explanation: Chapter 25 acknowledges delivery guarantees trade-offs. At-least-once with deduplication or idempotent writes is the pragmatic pattern for reliable pipelines when true exactly-once is costly or impossible.

questions.question: Which SLI is most appropriate for a batch analytics pipeline serving daily dashboards?
options: Data freshness: proportion of pipeline runs completing within N hours of source data availability
CPU utilization of the least-loaded worker
Number of lines in the pipeline configuration file
Count of engineers who can read the pipeline code
questions.explanation: Chapter 25 applies SRE thinking to pipelines: user-visible SLIs include freshness, completeness, and correctness — not just infrastructure metrics. Freshness lag directly affects dashboard trust.

questions.question: A pipeline stage rejects malformed records. Chapter 25-aligned handling stores them in a dead-letter path and:
options: Alerts on growing dead-letter volume, preserving good records flowing while enabling inspection and replay of bad data
Silently drops malformed records with no metrics
Halts the entire pipeline permanently on the first bad record
Re-injects malformed records unchanged into the output table
questions.explanation: Pipeline reliability separates poison records from healthy flow. Dead-letter queues with alerting mirror queue hygiene patterns while preserving data quality SLIs for downstream consumers.

questions.question: Chapter 26 advocates continuous data integrity verification because:
options: Silent corruption from hardware, software bugs, or migration errors can propagate undetected without proactive checksums and audits
Databases guarantee bitwise integrity forever once data is written
Integrity checks should only run during initial data import
Checksums reduce storage reliability by adding overhead
questions.explanation: Chapter 26 treats data integrity as an ongoing SRE responsibility: end-to-end checksums, periodic audits, and repair workflows catch corruption that replication alone may propagate.

questions.question: A checksum audit detects bit rot on one replica while others are healthy. The integrity-aligned response is:
options: Rebuild the corrupted replica from a verified good copy and investigate root cause before corruption spreads
Delete all replicas and restore from the oldest backup regardless of verification
Ignore single-replica corruption because quorum reads mask it
Disable checksums to reduce audit-induced load
questions.explanation: Chapter 26 describes detection-and-repair loops: verify, quarantine bad copies, rebuild from golden sources. Proactive repair prevents silent spread through replication and backup chains.

questions.question: During a large data migration, chapter 26-style integrity practice includes:
options: Dual-write or shadow-read validation comparing checksums and row counts between old and new stores before cutover
Cutover immediately after starting migration to minimize dual-system cost
Skipping validation for tables under 1 TB because corruption is unlikely
Migrating only a random 1% sample with no rollback plan
questions.explanation: Migration is a high-risk integrity window. Chapter 26 emphasizes verification gates — compare outputs, validate invariants, maintain rollback — before declaring migration complete.

questions.question: Launch readiness reviews in chapter 27 exist primarily to:
options: Verify operational preparedness — monitoring, SLOs, rollback, capacity, and on-call — before production traffic exposure
Replace all post-launch monitoring with a one-time sign-off
Guarantee zero bugs in new code before any testing
Shift all reliability responsibility from development to SRE permanently
questions.explanation: Chapter 27's reliable launches framework ensures services are operable at scale: defined SLOs, alerting, runbooks, capacity plans, and rollback paths — not just feature completeness.

questions.question: A launch checklist requires staged traffic ramp with automatic rollback hooks tied to SLO burn rate. This connects launch engineering to:
options: Error budget policy: stopping a bad launch before it consumes disproportionate budget across the full fleet
Eliminating the need for postmortems after failed launches
Deploying only during holidays when traffic is zero
Removing canary analysis in favor of big-bang releases
questions.explanation: Chapter 27 integrates launches with SRE principles: progressive exposure limits blast radius, SLO-linked rollback preserves error budget, and checklist items ensure the service is observable during ramp.
